purpose: Content bundle for a poetic, zoomable holarchy / panarchy simulation centered on a large old Mountain Ash (Eucalyptus regnans) tree in wet sclerophyll forest of the Central Highlands of Victoria. Focus is on the large old tree, hollows, standing-dead and fallen forms, associated flora and fauna, and the parameters needed for interactive world-building, evidence-aware simulation, and browser-based authoring.

anchoring_choice.primary_system: Mountain Ash / Eucalyptus regnans wet sclerophyll forest, Central Highlands of Victoria
anchoring_choice.focal_anchor: One hero large old hollow-bearing Mountain Ash tree inside a spatially faithful 1 ha old-growth wet sclerophyll patch with surrounding old trees, hollows, logs, tree ferns, understory, offspring/recruitment and dependent fauna.
anchoring_choice.why_this_system: Attached literature is strongest for Mountain Ash old growth, hollow-bearing tree monitoring, tree-decay trajectories, cavity-dependent vertebrates, bark streamers, tree ferns, bryophytes, large fallen timber, stand structural complexity, and panarchy framing.
anchoring_choice.user_requested_interface_bias: Poetic exploratory interface with a fully spatially faithful 3D world, adaptive semantic voxels of different sizes, particle trails instead of hard lines, browser-based authoring, and active weakly-evidenced links marked with an asterisk.

design_notes: This file is a content model, not a whole engine spec. It is designed to plug into a separate simulation kernel.
Positive and facilitative relations are emphasized, but disturbance relations are retained where they strongly shape the persistence of those positive relations.
Every parameter is labelled by evidence tier so literature-backed, synthesized, and guessed values can be separated during calibration.
Where the attached literature is Mountain-Ash-specific, values are stored as Mountain Ash preset defaults rather than universal eucalyptus truths.
Primary render metaphor is a spatially anchored 3D relation field: somewhere between terrestrial LiDAR, a living point cloud, and a network diagram.
As the camera zooms, nodes do not merely scale; they decompose into smaller parts, alter opacity, and reveal different action repertoires and parameters.
Weakly evidenced mechanisms remain active immediately, but every UI label for them should append an asterisk and expose the evidence tier in the inspector.
Browser-based authoring is a first-class requirement: the content bundle should be editable in-app, exportable as JSON, and internally versionable by patch history.
Structural complexity should remain multi-axis and inspectable rather than collapsed to a single score except in derived views.
Primary world view is now explicitly spatially faithful: entities remain anchored in metric world coordinates and are never force-drifted in the main scene.
The visual world is represented as an adaptive semantic voxel field rather than a dense uniform voxel grid. Voxel size varies with ecological scale, distance and role.
The canonical preset is now a one-hectare old-growth wet sclerophyll patch with one hero tree, surrounding old trees, hollows, fallen debris, offspring/recruitment field and associated beings.
Local-first is a hard requirement for MVP: all bundles, edits, scenarios and exports must work without a remote backend.

Relation rendering is controlled by a style grammar so different relation families can look linear, curving, wiggling, diffusing, pulsing or front-like while staying spatially anchored to source and destination surfaces.

attached_direct.description: Directly grounded in the uploaded Mountain Ash, large-old-tree, structural complexity, understory, or panarchy papers.

attached_synthesis.description: Synthesized from multiple uploaded sources but not a single direct measurement.

general_review_transfer.description: Transferred from broad review papers in the uploaded set and applicable to large old trees or tall eucalypt forests generally.

engine_placeholder.description: Simulation placeholder or tunable guess; replace during calibration.

weak_active_starred.meaning: Weakly evidenced or transferred mechanisms kept active in the simulation and visible by default, but clearly starred in labels.

entity_catalog.label: Bark streamer invertebrate guild
latin_names: flightless tree crickets and other bark-streamer invertebrates
entity_catalog.description: Weakly evidenced but ecologically plausible invertebrate assemblage associated with hanging bark microhabitats.

entity_catalog.label: Bark streamer patch
entity_catalog.description: Decorticating bark strips hanging from trunk or large lateral branches.

invertebrate_refuge_0_1.notes: Large old tree review flags bark micro-environments as under-studied but ecologically important.

fire_ladder_potential_0_1.notes: Long bark streamers can carry fire toward the canopy.

entity_catalog.label: Bryophyte / lichen guild
entity_catalog.description: Cryptogamic layer on logs, trunks, buttresses, rocks and tree ferns.

species_richness_average_per_site.notes: Average bryophyte richness exceeded 35 species per site in montane ash studies.

species_richness_high_per_site.notes: Some sites had over 50 species of bryophytes.

fire_refugia_dependence_0_1.notes: Persistence after fire depends strongly on intact microrefugia such as logs, tree ferns and sub-dominant trunks.

post_high_severity_survival_0_1.notes: High-severity sites were stripped to few pioneer species two years after fire.

entity_catalog.label: Bush Rat population
entity_catalog.description: Ground-dwelling mammal associated with understorey cover and fungal food resources.

entity_catalog.label: Buttress microhabitat
entity_catalog.description: Large basal flanges and associated bark/soil interfaces that hold moisture, support cryptogams, and modify access around the trunk.

entity_catalog.label: Canopy gap field
entity_catalog.description: Patchiness in canopy cover affecting light, temperature, moisture, and regeneration opportunities.

entity_catalog.label: Branch mat / fallen branch layer
entity_catalog.description: Fallen branches, bark and medium woody debris created by branch shedding, death and collapse.

fire_contagion_potential_0_1.notes: Dense self-thinning regrowth and branches can elevate fine and medium fuels.

entity_catalog.label: Crown / canopy module
entity_catalog.description: Canopy architecture and foliage arrangement relevant to light capture, bark streamers, flowering and fire behaviour.

vertical_layer_count.notes: Mountane ash stands can support overstorey, midstorey, shrub, tree fern and ground layers; this is a local canopy-context placeholder.

light_transmission_to_understory_0_1.notes: Old growth Mountain Ash crowns can allow substantial light penetration supporting multiple understory layers.

fire_ladder_potential_0_1.notes: Bark streamers and branch structure can increase crown-fire linkage.

entity_catalog.label: Dawsonia superba patch
entity_catalog.description: Tall moss patch characteristic of moist Mountain Ash forests; contributes to texture, moisture, and ground-layer identity.

entity_catalog.label: Drought regime
entity_catalog.description: Slow climatic stressor that increases mortality risk of large living hollow-bearing trees.

mortality_boost_large_old_trees_0_1.notes: Millennium Drought coincided with unexpected mortality on unburned sites.

interaction_with_productivity_0_1.notes: Large tree loss reflects tree, stand and landscape-level factors.

entity_catalog.label: Epiphytic plant guild
entity_catalog.description: Epiphytic plants associated with tree fern trunks, old understory plants and large old tree surfaces.

entity_catalog.label: Fallen log / large log
entity_catalog.description: Large fallen trunk or major branch that stores water, hosts fungi and bryophytes, and provides a germination platform.

moisture_retention_0_1.notes: Dense moss and lichen cover keeps underlying logs extremely wet.

nurse_regeneration_potential_0_1.notes: Rotting logs can provide nutrients and germination sites for ferns, bryophytes and rainforest trees.

nurse_log_quality_0_1.notes: Fallen timber provides ideal platform for ferns, bryophytes, and rainforest germination.

subsurface_wetness_0_1.notes: Dense bryophyte cover can keep underlying logs extremely wet.

entity_catalog.label: Wildfire / burn event
entity_catalog.description: Disturbance event with strong effects on large old trees, microrefugia, regeneration and substrate generation.

live_hollow_tree_kill_fraction_default.notes: On burned sites almost 80% of live hollow-bearing trees were killed.

dead_hollow_tree_destroy_fraction_max.notes: Between 58 and 100% of large old dead trees were destroyed by 2011.

microrefugia_persistence_0_1.notes: Unburned microrefugia can preserve bryophytes and some old structures.

legacy_creation_0_1.notes: Burned old growth can leave living fire-scarred and dead standing legacy trees.

entity_catalog.label: Fire scar patch
entity_catalog.description: Persistent trunk wound associated with past fire, altered bark thickness, and cavity initiation pathways.

entity_catalog.label: Fog moisture field
entity_catalog.description: Supplementary moisture source in montane ash forests through low-cloud condensation on tall canopies.

entity_catalog.label: Greater Glider population
entity_catalog.description: Folivorous glider associated with tall intact hollow trees and unburned forage access.

preferred_nest_height_m.notes: Often nests near the top of a living old growth tree, maybe ~60 m above the ground.

old_growth_density_multiplier.notes: Can reach population densities nearly three times greater in old growth than younger stands.

entity_catalog.label: Tree hollow / cavity
entity_catalog.description: Cavity within living or dead large old tree, formed through long-term decay processes, injury and structural aging.

host_tree_dbh_threshold_cm.notes: Monitoring defines a hollow-bearing tree as >80 cm DBH with an obvious hollow.

entrance_width_cm.notes: Keyhole hollows used by Leadbeater's Possum can be as narrow as 2-3 cm.

height_above_ground_m.notes: Species differ strongly: some hollows are a few metres high, others near 60 m.

rot_heat_index_0_1.notes: Decayed hollows may provide favorable nesting conditions for some possums.

occupancy_memory_years.notes: Some trees have been used by Leadbeater’s Possum for more than 10 years.

entity_catalog.label: Landscape memory field
entity_catalog.description: Cross-scale source of propagules, structures, and recovery pathways after disturbance.

entity_catalog.label: Large old Mountain Ash tree
entity_catalog.description: Primary organismal anchor. In the Mountain Ash preset this is a large old hollow-bearing tree in a multi-aged montane ash forest.

age_years.notes: Old growth Mountain Ash stands are mostly >180 years; decay-form figure includes 200-550+ year forms.

dbh_cm.notes: Old growth trees are typically >2 m DBH; hollow-bearing trees in monitoring defined as >80 cm with an obvious hollow.

height_m.notes: Tall old-growth and giant-tree contexts; species maximum exceeds this.

decay_form_index.notes: Mountain Ash hollow-bearing trees are described with nine forms from intact to collapsed.

bark_thickness_index_0_1.notes: Large old trees have thicker bark than younger trees and can be more fire resistant.

bark_streamer_load_0_1.notes: Old growth stands are characterized by large quantities of bark strips hanging from lateral branches.

buttress_extent_0_1.notes: Huge old Mountain Ash trees often have pronounced buttresses, especially on slopes.

mistletoe_load_0_1.notes: Old growth stands are characterized by well-developed clumps of mistletoe.

hollow_count_visible.notes: Many old growth trees contain visible hollows; count is a placeholder for scene instances.

carbon_storage_index_0_1.notes: Large old trees contribute disproportionately to stand carbon stocks.

microclimate_buffer_0_1.notes: Large old trees alter local microclimate through shade, canopy architecture, and water relations.

seed_source_strength_0_1.notes: Large old trees often act as reproductive dominants and nodes of regeneration.

fire_susceptibility_0_1.notes: Live large old trees can survive some fires, but many are killed in severe disturbance regimes.

drought_susceptibility_0_1.notes: Millennium Drought coincided with unexpected mortality of large living hollow-bearing trees.

crown_radius_m.notes: Approximate radius for a large old emergent crown in the focal scene.

crown_porosity_0_1.notes: Supports multi-layer light leakage into the wet sclerophyll understory.

fire_scar_count.notes: Very old trees can carry visible fire scars and thicker bark.

local_old_growth_signal_0_1.notes: Represents the tree as a keystone structural signal of old-growth conditions.

particle_emission_strength_0_1.notes: Controls particle trails drawn from this tree in the relation field.

zoom_decomposition_depth.notes: How many part layers this tree can unfold into during semantic zoom.

entity_catalog.label: Leadbeater's Possum colony
entity_catalog.description: Cavity-dependent marsupial colony strongly associated with large old hollow-bearing trees.

preferred_tree_height_max_m.notes: Typically favors large dead trees less than 30 m tall with a side hole.

kin_cohousing_under_scarcity_0_1.notes: When hollow-bearing trees are scarce, co-housing with close relatives increases.

entity_catalog.label: Logging matrix field
entity_catalog.description: Landscape configuration of harvested forest influencing fire severity, structural loss, and continuity of large old trees.

entity_catalog.label: Mistletoe clump
entity_catalog.description: Well-developed clumps in old-growth crowns; important structural and trophic elements.

entity_catalog.label: Mountain Brushtail Possum population
entity_catalog.description: Flexible hollow user and browser strongly linked to local hollow supply, tree ferns and some fungal food resources.

preferred_hollow_height_m.notes: Often uses hollows on dead sections of live trees only a few metres above the ground.

resource_scarcity_behavioral_shift_0_1.notes: Tree-choice preferences relax when suitable hollows are depleted.

entity_catalog.label: Mycorrhizal exchange network
entity_catalog.description: Starred because direct Mountain Ash network-scale transfer details are less constrained in the attached set, but belowground mycorrhizal coupling is strongly supported in the understory review.

entity_catalog.label: Old-growth patch
entity_catalog.description: Spatial patch containing high structural complexity, multiple ages, large living and dead trees, tree ferns, rainforest understorey and large fallen logs.

landscape_share_fraction.notes: Old growth Mountain Ash comprised ~1.16% of total area in the Central Highlands analysis.

entity_catalog.label: Rainforest understory tree and shrub guild
entity_catalog.description: Cool temperate rainforest and tall understory plants occurring beneath or among montane ash canopies.

bryophyte_refugia_value_0_1.notes: Sub-dominant trees such as Myrtle Beech can be key bryophyte refugia after fire.

old_understory_persistence_years.notes: Some old growth understory plants such as tree ferns can be ~350 years old in regrowth-dominated stands.

rainforest_break_effect_on_fire_0_1.notes: Rainforest understorey patches can reduce fire severity locally in tall eucalypt landscapes.

entity_catalog.label: Root mat
entity_catalog.description: Deep and extensive root system contributing to hydrology, nutrient uptake, and belowground structural coupling.

entity_catalog.label: Salvage logging / clearfell logging operation
entity_catalog.description: Human disturbance that removes standing and fallen structure, damages substrates and modifies fire risk.

tree_fern_loss_fraction.notes: Tree ferns can be reduced by up to 95% by logging operations.

substrate_removal_0_1.notes: Large logs, tree ferns and refugia are damaged or removed.

plant_richness_multiplier_after_salvage.notes: Plant species richness in one salvage-logged burned area was half that in unsalvaged burned locations.

future_fire_risk_boost_0_1.notes: Young logged regrowth is more prone to high-severity fire.

hollow_tree_buffer_radius_m.notes: Book recommends 100 m unlogged forest buffers around existing hollow-bearing trees.

entity_catalog.label: Saproxylic / wood-boring insect guild
entity_catalog.description: Insects dependent on stressed wood, dead wood or decay columns; central to late-wood food webs and microhabitat transformation.

fire_response_variability_0_1.notes: Mountain Ash fire work includes beetle responses but exact guild values remain to be tuned.

entity_catalog.label: Sooty Owl population
entity_catalog.description: Old-growth-associated owl linked to large structural elements and complex montane ash habitat.

entity_catalog.label: Spencer’s Skink population
entity_catalog.description: Skink associated with rocks, logs, forest floor, trunks, and even basking high in older tree canopies.

entity_catalog.label: Stand structural complexity field
entity_catalog.description: Derived, multi-attribute stand description tracking structure rather than a single universal index.

large_live_tree_density_per_ha.notes: Approximate historical hollow-tree abundance estimate used in projections.

deadwood_importance_weight.notes: Dead wood is a structural attribute comparable in importance to the overstorey.

understory_importance_weight.notes: Understory richness and cover are major structural complexity axes.

entity_catalog.label: Standing dead tree / snag
entity_catalog.description: Dead standing legacy structure derived from a formerly living tree; can retain hollows, branches and perch/roost/nest value for decades.

height_m.notes: Decay-form figure ranges from ~95 m intact forms to short pipes.

fire_vulnerability_0_1.notes: Old dead hollow-bearing trees are especially vulnerable to fire; many were consumed or piped irrespective of fire severity.

entity_catalog.label: Sunlight field
entity_catalog.description: External cyclic driver for diurnal and seasonal light delivery, spectral composition, and gap pulses.

entity_catalog.label: Tree fern guild
entity_catalog.description: Large understory tree ferns that host epiphytes, support regeneration on crowns, and provide food and microrefugia.

epiphyte_support_0_1.notes: Tree fern trunks support many epiphytic plants.

crown_germination_support_0_1.notes: Tree fern crowns are often locations for germination of rainforest trees.

browse_food_value_0_1.notes: Tree fern leaves/fiddleheads are food for Mountain Brushtail Possum.

fire_resilience_0_1.notes: Tree ferns are among the first plants to resprout after severe fire.

logging_reduction_max_fraction.notes: Logging operations can reduce populations of tree ferns by up to 95%.

entity_catalog.label: Wattle guild
entity_catalog.description: Post-fire and understory wattles that fix nitrogen and provide food resources for gliders and possums.

nitrogen_fixation_0_1.notes: Wattles are nitrogen-fixing plants and promote adjacent eucalypt growth.

gum_food_value_0_1.notes: Wattle gum is eaten by Leadbeater's Possums, Sugar Gliders and Yellow-bellied Gliders.

seed_pulse_after_fire_0_1.notes: Wattle seedlings can erupt as dense swarms after fire.

entity_catalog.label: Wood-decay fungi guild
entity_catalog.description: Fungi associated with living decay columns, standing dead trees and fallen logs; central to decomposition, nutrient cycling and later cavity formation.

post_fire_fruiting_speed_0_1.notes: Fungi emerged very quickly after the 2009 fires.

food_value_for_animals_0_1.notes: Some fungi are important food for Bush Rat and Mountain Brushtail Possum.

hollow_formation_contribution_0_1.notes: Cavity development in hardwood trees depends on slow-acting agents including fungi, termites and bacteria.

entity_catalog.label: Yellow-bellied Glider population
entity_catalog.description: Old-growth-associated glider linked to large, relatively intact hollow-bearing trees and wattle food resources.

entity_catalog.label: Mountain Ash recruitment cohort
entity_catalog.description: Juvenile and subadult Mountain Ash offspring/recruitment field surrounding older trees, representing regeneration, self-thinning and future canopy replacement.

initial_density_per_ha.notes: Scene-generation default for a mixed old-growth patch with limited local recruitment rather than dense even-aged post-fire regeneration.

light_dependence_0_1.notes: Understory regeneration in tall eucalypt systems is strongly structured by light heterogeneity, gap dynamics and competition.

fire_vulnerability_0_1.notes: Young Mountain Ash is highly vulnerable to repeated severe fire before replacement cohorts mature.

relation_catalog.description: Fungi decompose logs, opening later successional habitat while consuming the woody substrate.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Enzymatic decay and fruiting.

relation_catalog.description: Slow-acting fungal decay is one of the agents enabling cavity development in hardwood trees.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Heart rot and localized structural weakening over long time scales.

relation_catalog.description: Tree fern trunks support epiphytic plants.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Moist fibrous trunks and stable shaded surfaces.

relation_catalog.description: Tree fern crowns are frequent germination sites for rainforest plants.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Raised moist microsites and trapped organic matter.

relation_catalog.description: Tree fern leaves/fiddleheads are food for Mountain Brushtail Possum.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Leaf browsing.

relation_catalog.description: The abundance of large trees with hollows is a key determinant of cavity-dependent fauna persistence.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Provision of den and nest sites.

relation_catalog.description: Large intact hollow trees are key denning structures for Greater Glider.
relation_catalog.mechanism: High nest positions and access to surrounding canopy for gliding and foraging.

relation_catalog.description: Large old trees and their hollows support Mountain Brushtail Possum denning.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Access to flexible hollow types and nearby food plants.

relation_catalog.description: Tall relatively intact hollow-bearing trees serve as preferred nest structures for Yellow-bellied Glider.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Perch height, glide access and cavity availability.

relation_catalog.description: Sooty Owl is strongly associated with old growth montane ash stands.
relation_catalog.mechanism: High structural complexity, prey availability and old tree resources.

relation_catalog.description: Large old trees generate hollows through long periods of damage, decay and structural aging.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Age, heart rot, fungi, bacteria, termites, storm damage, fire scars and slow decay in hardwood stems.

relation_catalog.description: Yellow-bellied Glider is strongly associated with old growth stands.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Concentration of large old trees and old-growth food structure.

relation_catalog.description: Greater Glider population density is substantially higher in old growth than in younger stands.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Higher density and quality of nesting structures.

relation_catalog.description: Different hollow users sort among tree forms, heights and decay states rather than using the same cavity niche identically.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Species-specific preferences for dead/living trees, height and decay stage.

relation_catalog.description: Very narrow keyhole hollows can admit Leadbeater's Possum while excluding larger competitors.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Entrance diameter filter.

relation_catalog.description: Wildfire kills many live large old hollow-bearing trees.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Crown scorch/consumption, cambial damage and heat stress.

relation_catalog.description: Dead hollow-bearing trees are especially vulnerable to fire and may be consumed or piped.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Combustion of decayed wood and chimney formation.

relation_catalog.description: Fire can simultaneously destroy old structures and create fire-scarred living/dead trees plus unburned microrefugia that shape later recolonisation.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Partial survival, patchiness and disturbance legacies.

relation_catalog.description: Unburned or lightly affected structures and patches act as microrefugia for bryophytes after fire.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Retention of suitable logs, sub-dominant trunks, tree ferns and moisture conditions.

relation_catalog.description: Salvage logging damages refugia and removes large logs and tree ferns needed by bryophytes.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Mechanical disturbance and extraction of structural substrates.

relation_catalog.description: Logging and salvage logging sharply reduce tree fern abundance.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Mechanical disturbance, burning and removal.

relation_catalog.description: Old trees and buttresses provide bark and moisture microhabitats for mosses, lichens, liverworts and hornworts.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Large diameter, old bark, rough surfaces, persistent humidity, and structural shelter.

relation_catalog.description: Post-fire wattles help promote the growth of adjacent eucalypts.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Nitrogen fixation and early successional support.

relation_catalog.description: Wattle gum feeds Yellow-bellied Gliders; leaves and seeds are used by other marsupials.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Gum, leaves and seeds as seasonal foods.

relation_catalog.description: Some fungi are food for Bush Rat and Mountain Brushtail Possum, which in turn can disperse spores.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Consumption of fungal fruit bodies and fecal dispersal.

relation_catalog.description: Large old trees disproportionately contribute to biomass carbon and to structural complexity.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Large size, longevity and retention as deadwood after disturbance.

relation_catalog.description: Old growth patches concentrate large live trees, dead cavity trees, bark streamers, tree ferns, rainforest understory and large fallen logs.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Long interval since major stand-replacing disturbance and accumulated legacies.

relation_catalog.description: Extended drought increases mortality of large living hollow-bearing trees even on unburned sites.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Hydraulic stress and interacting biotic/abiotic decline factors.

relation_catalog.description: Logging and regenerated young stands can elevate the risk of later high-severity fire, threatening future structural legacies.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Dense regrowth, fuel accumulation, canopy connectivity and slash dynamics.

relation_catalog.description: Very old understory elements can persist beneath younger overstorey and carry ecological memory across stand phases.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Long-lived understory organisms surviving repeated disturbance and supporting recolonisation.

relation_catalog.description: Tree, hollow, snag, log, stand and landscape legacies are nested across scales and move through growth, conservation, release and reorganization phases.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Nested adaptive cycles and scale breaks; large old trees act as memory sources during reorganization.

relation_catalog.description: Hanging bark patches likely shelter a rich invertebrate assemblage, but direct Mountain Ash quantification remains limited.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Humidity buffering, predation refuge, textured surfaces, and suspended bark chambers.

relation_catalog.description: Old growth trees carry extensive bark streamers that create hanging microhabitats and fire ladders.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Aging bark architecture and branch structure.

relation_catalog.description: Long bark strips can help carry fire upward into the canopy.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Suspended combustible material bridges vertical layers.

relation_catalog.display_label: fallen logs hold water for cryptogams
relation_catalog.description: Bryophyte-covered logs remain extremely wet and support mosses, lichens, ferns, and seedlings.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Rotting wood plus dense cryptogam cover create a persistent moisture reservoir.

relation_catalog.display_label: large old tree supports Spencer’s Skink
relation_catalog.description: Older trees and associated structures provide basking and movement habitat for Spencer’s Skink.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Trunks, canopy surfaces, logs, and thermal heterogeneity.

relation_catalog.description: Old-growth crowns often carry well-developed mistletoe clumps.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Branch structure and long continuity permit mistletoe establishment.

relation_catalog.display_label: tree ferns carry memory through fire
relation_catalog.description: Tree ferns often survive fire and preserve old-forest continuity through the release and reorganization phases.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Resprouting from the top of the trunk and long persistence.

relation_catalog.description: Canopy gaps alter light, moisture, and temperature and thereby restructure understory opportunities.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Gap fraction changes light quality and water availability across layers.

relation_catalog.description: Dense understory alters temperature, moisture, litter decomposition, and nutrient mineralization near the tree.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Surface shading, litter input, organic matter processing, and root activity.

relation_catalog.description: Belowground root interactions and mycorrhizal networks likely mediate transfer of resources and information.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Shared fungal interfaces extend explored soil volume and shape resource exchange.

relation_catalog.description: Network-scale mycorrhizal exchange is kept active as a starred mechanism for resource and stress buffering.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Potential redistribution of limiting resources and partial alleviation of abiotic stress.

relation_catalog.display_label: fog adds moisture to tall canopy
relation_catalog.description: Low-cloud condensation supplements moisture inputs in montane ash forest.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Condensation on leaves and tall surfaces augments wet conditions.

relation_catalog.description: Very large old trees form buttresses that create microtopography and substrate complexity.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Mechanical support in giant trees, especially on slopes.

relation_catalog.description: Daily and seasonal light is filtered by canopy architecture, yielding heterogeneous understory pulses.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Solar angle and canopy cover interact to produce patchy illumination.

relation_catalog.description: Harvesting truncates recruitment of large old trees and erodes structural continuity.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Removal before ecological maturity and landscape fragmentation.

relation_catalog.description: Legacy structures, old trees, and understory survivors act as memory during post-disturbance reorganization.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Retention of structures, propagules, microhabitats, and surviving organisms.

relation_catalog.display_label: old growth patch enriches structural complexity
relation_catalog.description: Old-growth patches concentrate multi-aged trees, canopy variation, deadwood, tree ferns, and cavity trees.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Long disturbance intervals and persistence of large old structures.

relation_catalog.description: As tree form changes from intact to decayed to pipe to log, preferred cavity users change.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Changes in height, decay, access, thermal conditions, and glide utility.

relation_catalog.description: Cryptogam cover increases water retention and helps maintain moist nurse-log conditions.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Dense cover with substantial water-holding capacity.

relation_catalog.description: When a living large old tree dies, it often persists as a standing dead tree that retains habitat value.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Drought, fire, senescence, structural failure.

relation_catalog.description: Standing dead trees eventually collapse and become large logs with new substrate roles.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Decay, windthrow, combustion, structural failure.

relation_catalog.description: Fallen timber provides ideal wet substrate for bryophytes and lichens.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Rotting wood plus water-holding cover keeps the log surface wet.

relation_catalog.description: Rotting logs provide germination platforms and nutrients for ferns and rainforest trees.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Moisture retention, nutrient release, reduced ground competition.

relation_catalog.description: Fallen timber hosts fungi that drive decomposition and nutrient cycling.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Wood substrate with sufficient moisture and carbon resources.

relation_catalog.description: Large old Mountain Ash contributes seed rain and genetic continuity to nearby recruitment, though successful establishment depends on light, substrate, moisture and disturbance history.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Flowering, seed production, dispersal and establishment into microsites.

relation_catalog.description: The structural legacy of old trees, hollows, logs, tree ferns and remnant patches acts as ecological memory for later reorganization after disturbance.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Persistence of legacies, propagules, substrates and habitat structures across disturbance events.

relation_catalog.description: The crown and stem architecture of the hero tree locally reduces light, intercepts rainfall and modulates understory microclimate, which can facilitate some understory species while suppressing others.
relation_catalog.mechanism: Shading, rainfall interception, litter and structural heterogeneity.

derived_metrics.description: Keep stand structure as separable axes rather than collapsing early into one score.
axes: dead wood: standing dead trees, logs, litter

derived_metrics.description: Track the life-history sequence of a large old tree through living, dead standing, piped and collapsed log states.
Map leaf/organ fast loops, tree slow loops, hollow century-scale persistence, stand fire-release events, and landscape memory/reorganization.
Old-growth Mountain Ash signature based on attached criteria rather than a single score.
criteria: many living and dead cavity trees

derived_metrics.description: Render relations as particle trails and nodes as point-cloud / semi-voxel presences whose opacity and decomposition vary with zoom band.

visual_presets.label: Poetic point-cloud world
visual_presets.description: Spatially faithful 3D scene with adaptive semantic voxels, instanced points/billboards/boxes, translucent bodies, and soft particle trails instead of hard edges.

rules.weak_relation_visual: same functionality, star badge in labels and provenance inspector
rules.zoom_opacity_curve: entities are most opaque and most editable near their home scale and fade outside it
rules.decomposition_rule: on zoom-in, organism -> parts -> microhabitats -> tissue surfaces; on zoom-out, organism -> stand role -> landscape memory role
rules.surface_anchor_rule: trails spawn and terminate on explicit source/target anchors such as cavity rim, bark surface, canopy shell, root zone or log face
rules.world_coordinates_rule: entity transforms remain in metric plot coordinates; trails and mobile fauna move, but fixed nodes do not drift

visual_presets.label: Orthographic relation debug
visual_presets.description: Secondary analysis view for inspecting typed edges, evidence tiers, and parameter magnitudes.

visual_presets.label: Panarchy cycle view
visual_presets.description: Nested cycle timeline emphasizing memory, revolt, release, and reorganization.

open_research_questions: How should saproxylic insect guilds be parameterized in Mountain Ash specifically, rather than by generalized deadwood guild placeholders?
Which epiphyte taxa should be modeled explicitly in montane ash versus as a guild?
How much spatial detail is needed to represent hollow competition and kin-based co-housing in Leadbeater's Possum?
Should the first playable preset remain Mountain Ash, or should a second Messmate / Warra preset be added in parallel?
Which structural complexity axes should be editable by end users, and which should remain derived only?
How much of bark-streamer invertebrate life can be directly quantified in Mountain Ash rather than inferred from general large-old-tree literature?
What are the best parameterizations for multi-layer point-cloud decomposition so ecological parts feel meaningful rather than decorative?
How should old-growth memory sources be translated into executable panarchy remember / revolt parameters for simulation?
Which subset of weak-active-starred mechanisms should be promoted to stronger evidence tiers after the next literature pass?

source_catalog.title: Lindenmayer et al. (2015) Mountain Ash: Fire, Logging and the Future of Victoria’s Giant Forests
used_for: tree ferns, bryophytes and fungi on logs
fire and drought impacts on large old trees

source_catalog.title: Lindenmayer & Laurance (2017) The ecology, distribution, conservation and management of large old trees
Lindenmayer (2017) Conserving large old trees as small natural features
McElhinny et al. (2005) Forest and woodland stand structural complexity: Its definition and measurement
Deng et al. (2023) Forest understory vegetation study: current status and future trends
Sundstrom et al. (2023) Panarchy theory for convergence
User interface and interaction preferences supplied in chat

authoring_hints.primary_workflow: parameter editing of a richly preloaded relation set
authoring_hints.secondary_workflow: creation of new entities or relations where the model is still missing something
authoring_hints.edit_safety: every field should retain evidence tier, notes, and original default
authoring_hints.weak_link_policy: weak_active_starred relations remain executable by default and are visually starred

world_model.spatial_faithfulness_rule: All fixed ecological entities remain anchored in metric world coordinates in the primary view. No force-directed drift is permitted for trees, hollows, logs, ferns or patch features. Mobile fauna, particles, weather fields and growth transforms can move.
world_model.camera_rule: camera movement changes distance and scale emphasis but not the underlying position of ecological entities
world_model.notes: This is intended to feel like a hybrid between a LiDAR point cloud, a living voxel world and a relational particle field.

relation_style_library.notes: Use for water, nutrients, propagules and pollen-like motions.
Use for VOC plumes, alarm fields and diffuse attractants.
Use for repeated movement between an organism and a hollow or nest site.
Use for owl strikes, parasitoid approaches and other directed pursuits.
Use for fungal spread, decay fronts and starred network-like belowground couplings.
Use for wildfire or prescribed-fire visualizations across the voxel field.

world_presets.label: 1 ha old-growth Mountain Ash wet sclerophyll patch
world_presets.description: Default local world for the MVP: one 100 m x 100 m plot with one hero large old tree, supporting old trees, dead hollow trees, large fallen logs, tree ferns, rainforest understory, bryophyte-rich substrates and a small dependent fauna set.

cohort_defaults.label: supporting living old trees
cohort_defaults.notes: Derived as a playable subset of old-growth structure rather than a census.

cohort_defaults.label: standing dead hollow trees
cohort_defaults.notes: Total living + dead hollow-bearing trees should be able to exceed 30/ha in old-growth settings.

recent_logging_years_ago.notes: Null in the canonical unlogged old-growth preset.

spawn_notes: Keep one visually dominant hero tree near the plot centre.
Bias tree ferns, bryophytes and rainforest understory toward gullies, logs and shaded sectors.
Place some hollows high in intact living trees and others lower in shorter, more decayed stems to support different fauna.
Use large fallen timber as both structure and moisture-retaining substrate rather than decorative debris only.
Allow the user to tune all cohort counts and habitat intensities from the browser.

local_first_runtime.sharing: manual file export/import only in MVP
local_first_runtime.notes: The project should run entirely on a local machine until interaction, content and simulation feel stable enough for broader deployment.

performance_profiles.notes: Performance target for spatially faithful adaptive-voxel rendering with particle trails.
